metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add run_differs.py lines 15-17 to the comment-update scope: the docstring phrase 'which reconcile.py keeps because it is a separately-tested public surface' must be updated to reflect that route_inbound_probe now lives in reconcile_helpers.py and is re-exported from reconcile.py.
evidence: run_differs.py line 15-17: 'the probe router (``route_inbound_probe``, which reconcile.py keeps because it is a separately-tested public surface) is passed in as a parameter'
Plan 'Seam' section: 'The stale reconcile.py comment near current line 586 ... is UPDATED' — only reconcile.py comment mentioned, run_differs.py omitted
Grep confirmed the stale text exists at run_differs.py lines 15-17
findings.finding: The plan states the stale comment to update is the one 'near current line 586' of reconcile.py, but run_differs.py lines 15-17 contain an equally stale — and now false — assertion that 'route_inbound_probe, which reconcile.py keeps because it is a separately-tested public surface, is passed in as a parameter.' This is a codebase assertion the plan fails to include in its update scope. After the move, run_differs.py's docstring will incorrectly describe where route_inbound_probe lives.
scenarios: After the split, run_differs.py's module docstring falsely claims route_inbound_probe lives in reconcile.py, confusing future readers and anyone grep-ing for the canonical location of route_inbound_probe.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Seam section, extend the comment-update bullet to: 'The stale comment near reconcile.py line 586 AND the run_differs.py module docstring (lines 15-17) are UPDATED to reflect that route_inbound_probe now lives in reconcile_helpers.py and is re-exported from reconcile.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `run_differs.py` to the edit-target list and update its module docstring (lines 15-17) to reflect that `route_inbound_probe` now lives in `reconcile_helpers.py` and is re-exported through `reconcile.py`.
evidence: Plan Seam section: 'The stale reconcile.py comment near current line 586 ... is UPDATED to reflect that route_inbound_probe now lives in the leaf and is re-exported'

run_differs.py line 15-17: 'It holds NO back-edge to reconcile.py: the probe router (route_inbound_probe, which reconcile.py keeps because it is a separately-tested public surface) is passed in as a parameter'
run_differs.py is not mentioned anywhere in the plan as requiring an edit
findings.finding: The plan enumerates updating the stale reconcile.py comment at ~line 586 (about route_inbound_probe staying in reconcile.py), but does NOT enumerate updating the run_differs.py module-level docstring (lines 15-17), which explicitly states: 'the probe router (route_inbound_probe, which reconcile.py keeps because it is a separately-tested public surface)'. After the move, this statement becomes false — route_inbound_probe lives in reconcile_helpers.py, not reconcile.py — yet run_differs.py is not listed as an edit target.
scenarios: An executor applies the plan exactly as written: reconcile.py's comment is updated, but run_differs.py's docstring still claims route_inbound_probe 'reconcile.py keeps' — a false statement that misleads future readers about the module's ownership.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Seam section, after the sentence about updating the stale reconcile.py comment, add: 'The run_differs.py module docstring (currently lines 15-17) is also updated to remove the parenthetical stating route_inbound_probe "reconcile.py keeps".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with a proving grep command) that verifies the stale comment near line 586 no longer contains the old text, e.g.: `grep -c 'stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` returns 0.
evidence: Plan states: 'The stale reconcile.py comment near current line 586 ("route_inbound_probe stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface") is UPDATED to reflect that route_inbound_probe now lives in the leaf and is re-exported'
No AC contains a grep or diff command verifying this comment change was applied.
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT type (4): a described required change with no corresponding proving command.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly requires updating the stale comment near line 586 of reconcile.py ('is UPDATED to reflect that route_inbound_probe now lives in the leaf and is re-exported'), but no acceptance criterion verifies that this update was made.
findings.location: Seam section — 'The stale reconcile.py comment near current line 586 … is UPDATED'
scenarios: An executor completes all ACs (tests pass, lint clean, LOC within bounds) but forgets to update the comment; the plan has no mechanism to catch the omission.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '- [ ] The stale comment about route_inbound_probe is updated. Verified by: `grep -c "stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface" src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` exits 0 (count = 0).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC criterion (or extend an existing one) to verify the stale line-586 comment is updated, e.g.: `grep -c 'route_inbound_probe stays here' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` returns 0.
evidence: Seam: 'The stale reconcile.py comment near current line 586 (\'route_inbound_probe stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface\') is UPDATED to reflect that route_inbound_probe now lives in the leaf and is re-exported'
No AC criterion references this comment update; lint and unit tests would not detect a missing comment update.
findings.finding: The Seam section describes a concrete deliverable — updating the stale comment near reconcile.py line 586 ('route_inbound_probe stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface') to reflect the new location — but no AC criterion verifies that this comment update was performed.
findings.location: Seam section ('The stale reconcile.py comment near current line 586 … is UPDATED to reflect that route_inbound_probe now lives in the leaf and is re-exported') vs. AC criteria 1–7
scenarios: Implementer forgets to update the comment; the stale comment misleads future readers and contradicts the new module structure, but all AC criteria pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: '- [ ] The stale comment `route_inbound_probe stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface` is removed/updated. Verified by: `grep -c "route_inbound_probe stays here" src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` returns 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC3 and AC4 proving commands to unambiguously assert COUNT=0, either by checking printed output (`grep -c pattern file | grep -qx '0'`) or by using two separate `grep -q -v`/`grep -c` assertions, and add a note clarifying that the expected PRINTED count is 0 (not the exit code).
evidence: "grep -c 'reconcile.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` returns 0" — grep -c exits 0 when match count ≥ 1, exits 1 when count = 0; so exit-code 0 means the pattern WAS found, which is the failure state

"grep -c reconcile_helpers .github/module-size-allowlist.txt .github/module-size-ceilings.txt` returns 0 for both" — a single multi-file grep exits 0 if any file matches, making per-file 0-count unverifiable from exit code alone
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT type (1): wrong exit-code semantics for an absence check
findings.finding: AC3 and AC4's grep-based proving commands use 'returns 0' ambiguously: `grep -c` exits 0 when the pattern IS found (bad outcome for a 'must be absent' check) and exits 1 when not found (good outcome). An executor following exit-code semantics would interpret a FAILED check as success. The author likely intends the printed count to be 0, but this is not explicitly stated, and AC4's multi-file form ('returns 0 for both') cannot be asserted via exit code alone since a single multi-file grep exits 0 if either file matches.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('grep -c returns 0') and AC4 ('returns 0 for both')
scenarios: An executor interprets 'returns 0' as exit-code 0 = success and concludes the gate files are clean even when reconcile.py is still listed.
An executor running the multi-file AC4 command cannot distinguish 'allowlist has match, ceilings does not' from 'neither has a match' via exit code.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace each 'returns 0' grep with an explicit count assertion, e.g.:
```
# AC3
[ "$(grep -c 'reconcile.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt)" -eq 0 ]
[ "$(grep -c 'rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py ' .github/module-size-ceilings.txt)" -eq 0 ]
[ "$(grep -c 'rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py' docs/architecture.md)" -eq 0 ]
# AC4
[ "$(grep -c reconcile_helpers .github/module-size-allowlist.txt)" -eq 0 ]
[ "$(grep -c reconcile_helpers .github/module-size-ceilings.txt)" -eq 0 ]
```

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean

verify command `grep -c reconcile_helpers .github/module-size-allowlist.txt .github/module-size-ceilings.txt` greps the unanchored word 'reconcile_helpers' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_reconcile_helpers'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"reconcile_helpers":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC criterion 3's verify to target the remedy row specifically (e.g., grep for the remedy-row pattern such as `grep -c 'reconcile.py.*remedy\|remedy.*reconcile.py' docs/architecture.md` returns 0, or anchor to the offenders-table context) rather than any occurrence of the path string.
evidence: DEVIATION: 'This ticket DELETES the stale reconcile.py remedy row from docs/architecture.md'
AC criterion 3 verify: 'grep -c \'rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py\' docs/architecture.md` returns 0'
The verify checks for any occurrence of the path, not specifically the remedy row.
findings.finding: The AC criterion 3 verify command (`grep -c 'rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py' docs/architecture.md` returns 0) requires ZERO occurrences of the path anywhere in the file, but the DEVIATION section only commits to deleting 'the stale reconcile.py remedy row' — if architecture.md mentions reconcile.py in any narrative, header, or non-remedy context, the verify fails even though the described work is complete. This is a contradiction between the scoped intent and the verification method.
findings.location: AC criterion 3 verify step vs. DEVIATION section ('DELETES the stale reconcile.py remedy row from docs/architecture.md')
scenarios: architecture.md contains a narrative sentence like 'The reconciler package consists of reconcile.py, run_differs.py …' — the grep returns nonzero and CI fails even though the remedy row was correctly deleted.
An implementer scrubs ALL mentions of reconcile.py from architecture.md to satisfy the grep, inadvertently deleting valid architectural documentation beyond the remedy row.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the docs verify with a command that checks the offenders/remedy table context specifically, e.g.: `grep -c 'reconcile\.py' docs/architecture.md` may still be fine IF the plan is updated to say 'all mentions are removed,' OR change the plan text to say 'the remedy row AND any offenders-table entry for reconcile.py are removed' and adjust the grep to match only table rows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note to docs/architecture.md (e.g., in a 'Resolved' sub-section or inline comment in the offenders table) recording that reconcile.py was graduated via leaf-utility extraction into reconcile_helpers.py and why the phase-helper seam was not used, so the rationale survives beyond this ticket.
evidence: 'This ticket DELETES the stale reconcile.py remedy row from docs/architecture.md'
No corresponding plan step adds a resolved-state note or rationale stub to architecture.md
The DEVIATION section explains WHY only in the ticket text, not in a durable doc artifact
findings.finding: The plan deletes the reconcile.py remedy row from docs/architecture.md without adding any replacement entry or rationale note explaining the chosen seam (leaf-utility extraction vs. the superseded phase-helper seam). Future contributors consulting architecture.md will see the resolved state but lose the architectural rationale that justified deviating from the original remedy.
findings.location: DEVIATION section; AC criterion 3 (`grep -c 'rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py' docs/architecture.md` returns 0)
scenarios: A future contributor reopens the 'graduate reconcile.py' work item, finds no trace in architecture.md of why leaf-utility extraction was chosen over phase-helper extraction, and re-proposes the original (problematic) seam.
An onboarding engineer reads architecture.md to understand module-size remediation patterns and sees no example of how the largest offender was resolved.
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/architecture.md, rather than only deleting the reconcile.py row, add a sentence in the surrounding context (e.g., a 'Graduated modules' note or a prose paragraph near the offenders table): 'reconcile.py (resolved): graduated by extracting 10 pure/IO leaf utilities into reconcile_helpers.py; the originally-proposed phase-helper seam (reconcile_passes.py) was deferred because it would have required a three-module split to avoid a back-edge into reconcile.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `StatusMappingError` in `reconcile_helpers.py` should re-export `_errors.StatusMappingError` (already the canonical copy consumed by `apply_base.py`) rather than defining a third independent class, to avoid class-identity divergence across `isinstance` boundaries.
evidence: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_errors.py:126: class StatusMappingError(Exception): — canonical definition already exists
src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_base.py:99: StatusMappingError = _errors_module.StatusMappingError — already consumes _errors copy
src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py:22: class StatusMappingError(Exception): — duplicate definition the plan moves to the new leaf
Plan states 'StatusMappingError' is one of the 10 moved symbols, with no note about consolidation with _errors.py
findings.finding: The plan silently carries forward a duplicate `StatusMappingError` definition into the new leaf. `_errors.py` already defines `StatusMappingError` (line 126) and `apply_base.py` already resolves it from there (line 99). `reconcile.py` defines a second, independent copy (line 22) — moving it into `reconcile_helpers.py` perpetuates the duplication rather than consolidating on `_errors.StatusMappingError`. The plan's description of the 10 moved symbols presents `StatusMappingError` as a straightforward move, with no acknowledgment of the pre-existing canonical copy in `_errors.py`.
findings.location: Seam section ('10 moved symbols … StatusMappingError') and reconcile.py:22 vs _errors.py:126
scenarios: A test that imports `reconcile.StatusMappingError` and one that imports `applier.StatusMappingError` (which comes from `_errors.py`) will get two different class objects — `isinstance` checks across the boundary will silently fail.
The duplication grows more entrenched as reconcile_helpers.py becomes an established module, making future consolidation harder.
findings.suggested_fix: In `reconcile_helpers.py`, instead of copying the class body, import and re-export the existing canonical: `from rebar_reconciler._errors import StatusMappingError` (with the same lazy_load pattern the module uses for other siblings). Then `reconcile.py`'s re-export of `StatusMappingError` will point to the same class object as `apply_base.py`'s.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that `StatusMappingError` has a parallel definition in `_errors.py` (used by `apply_base.py`), and explicitly state that the moved copy in `reconcile_helpers.py` remains a separate class (not unified with `_errors.StatusMappingError`), so readers understand there are intentionally two distinct error classes with this name.
evidence: _errors.py line 126: 'class StatusMappingError(Exception):'
apply_base.py line 99: 'StatusMappingError = _errors_module.StatusMappingError'
reconcile.py line 22: 'class StatusMappingError(Exception):' — a separate definition
reconcile.py lines 27-30: 'The class stays defined for references (e.g. tests) that still name it' — plan intends to move this class
test_errors.py line 69: 'assert issubclass(errs.StatusMappingError, Exception)' — tests the _errors.py version independently
findings.finding: The plan claims `StatusMappingError` is one of the 10 symbols moved from reconcile.py. However, `StatusMappingError` ALSO exists as a separately-defined class in `_errors.py` (line 126), and `apply_base.py` imports it from there (line 99: `StatusMappingError = _errors_module.StatusMappingError`). These are two distinct class objects. The plan does not address this duality. After the move, there will be THREE class objects with the same name (reconcile.StatusMappingError re-exported from reconcile_helpers.StatusMappingError, plus _errors.StatusMappingError), and any `isinstance` check or `except` clause that catches one will not catch instances of the other.
findings.location: Seam section / reconcile.py line 22, _errors.py line 126, apply_base.py line 99
scenarios: Post-split, code catching `reconcile.StatusMappingError` (re-exported from reconcile_helpers) won't catch instances raised as `_errors.StatusMappingError` (from apply_base), and vice versa — silent miss if any call site mixes the two.

The plan's description of StatusMappingError as a pure leaf utility glosses over the fact that the same name already exists in _errors.py, creating a class-identity ambiguity the plan should explicitly resolve.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `_commit_binding_store_snapshot`'s `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter` must be converted to `_load('rebar_reconciler.git_adapter', 'git_adapter.py')` (or equivalent `lazy_load` call) in the new leaf, consistent with the package's standalone-load convention used by all other sibling modules.
evidence: reconcile.py line 162: `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter` — a standard package import inside _commit_binding_store_snapshot body
Plan claims leaf 'lazy-loads ... git_adapter' but does not say 'convert the package import to a _load() call'
run_differs.py and all other standalone-load-compatible siblings use _load() for every sibling dependency, never bare package imports
test_commit_binding_store_failure.py loads reconcile.py standalone via spec_from_file_location — the same pattern that would be applied to reconcile_helpers.py
findings.finding: The plan lists `git_adapter` among the siblings the leaf 'lazy-loads' but `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` — one of the 10 moved symbols — currently imports it via `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter` (a standard package import inside the function body). If the implementer moves this function literally, standalone test loads of `reconcile_helpers.py` (the dominant test pattern: `spec_from_file_location` without package context) will raise `ModuleNotFoundError` for `rebar_reconciler`, breaking every test that loads the new leaf directly or exercises `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` through it. Converting to `_load('rebar_reconciler.git_adapter', 'git_adapter.py')` is the non-obvious fix — it is not spelled out in the plan despite being the only mechanism compatible with the package's standalone-load test convention.
findings.location: Seam section — 'The leaf reproduces the lazy_load+_load bootstrap boilerplate ... lazy-loads ITS OWN siblings (config.py, alert_store.py, mutation.py, inbound_probe.py, git_adapter)'
scenarios: Implementer copies _commit_binding_store_snapshot verbatim into reconcile_helpers.py; any test that loads reconcile_helpers.py standalone (or imports it indirectly via the re-export path in a non-package context) hits `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'rebar_reconciler'` at the `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter` line.

The reconciler unit suite (AC6) stays green only if the test infrastructure happens to have the full package on sys.path — not guaranteed in the test harness that deliberately tests standalone-load isolation.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Seam section, add: 'Note: `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` currently uses `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter`; this must be converted to `git_adapter = _load("rebar_reconciler.git_adapter", "git_adapter.py")` inside the function body (or as a module-level lazy load) so the leaf remains standalone-loadable without package context, matching the convention in run_differs.py.'

findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.
